summary:
The origins of pastoralism — the herding of domesticated livestock as a primary subsistence strategy — in East Africa have been traced by archaeological and genetic evidence to the period following the onset of the [[event:17|African Humid Period]] around 10,000 BCE, when improved climatic conditions expanded the grasslands and water availability of the East African interior. Early cattle domestication in Africa appears to have occurred independently from or very shortly after Near Eastern cattle domestication, with the earliest evidence of managed cattle in the region of the eastern Sahara and Nile Valley dating to approximately 9,000–7,000 BCE.

The spread of pastoralism across East and sub-Saharan Africa during the early to mid-Holocene was a major demographic and economic process. Herding communities spread the management of cattle, sheep, and goats across the continent's grassland and savanna environments, carrying with them cultural practices, genetic lineages, and subsistence strategies that would shape African societies for millennia. Sites associated with early East African pastoralism document the use of livestock long before any evidence of crop cultivation in the region, reflecting the distinctive path of African subsistence development — pastoralism without agriculture — that persisted and remained dominant across much of the East African interior until recent centuries.
